O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — STATUS: MULTIPLE BREACHES | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The supply chain risk profile is at its most acute combination of concentration, coverage, and counterparty stress seen in the current data. Single-source concentration has reached 62.6% of $12,610M total spend, representing a breach of the 50% threshold and well above the amber threshold of 40%. Three suppliers — TSMC ($4,200M, Semiconductors-CPU), Foxconn ($2,100M, Manufacturing-Assembly), and Quanta ($1,600M, ODM-Laptops) — account for $7,900M of spend with no alternative source.

The most acute risk sits with Quanta [SUP005]. Its financial health score of 61 is below the distress flag threshold of 65, its inventory cover of 3.9 weeks is below the 4-week breach threshold, and its lead time is 6 weeks — meaning that if Quanta were to experience an operational or financial disruption today, the organisation would exhaust existing inventory before any alternative arrangement could be activated. This constitutes the dual escalation trigger (financial_health < 65 AND inventory < 4 weeks simultaneously). Quanta's OTD of 88.7% is already the weakest among single-source suppliers, and its rejection rate of 1.2% is the highest in the supply base.

Yangtze Memory [SUP008], a multi-source supplier, also carries a financial health score of 55 — the lowest in the portfolio — though its multi-source status partially mitigates the continuity risk.

TSMC represents a concentration risk of a different character: $4,200M of single-source spend with an 18-week lead time, in a country (Taiwan) that already accounts for 53.5% of total geographic exposure. The supply chain VaR model outputs $9.1B revenue at risk and a $2,634M 95th-percentile loss scenario, with a 5-month recovery timeline. A Taiwan-specific disruption scenario (geopolitical, seismic, or export control) would be unrecoverable from existing inventory within the TSMC lead time window.

The stress model identifies dual-sourcing as the highest-value near-term mitigation at $382M in potential savings, and inventory buffer optimisation at $305M. A 24% probability of demand shock with 15% impact is also flagged. The EBITDA exposure from premium sourcing during a disruption, combined with the 5-month recovery period, would be material but is not separately quantified beyond the VaR figures provided.

O-02 CYBER AND IT — STATUS: MULTIPLE BREACHES | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

The cyber risk posture is deteriorating across detection, response, vulnerability management, and third-party coverage simultaneously.

Mean Time to Detect has reached 11 days against a breach threshold of 10 days, and Mean Time to Respond is 18 days. This means the combined window from incident initiation to resolution can approach 29 days. With 4 security incidents recorded month-to-date and 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days, the organisation is operating in a sustained state of unresolved exposure.

Patch compliance at 71% is below the 80% breach threshold. With 8 critical and 23 high vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days, the attack surface is both broad and persistent. Privileged access accounts have gone unreviewed for 45 days, representing a significant lateral movement risk if any of the current security incidents involve credential compromise.

The single most urgent finding is supplier_cyber_resilience_assess_pct at 0%. No supplier in the portfolio has been assessed for cyber resilience, and 4 security incidents have occurred this month. This meets the escalation trigger directly. Given that TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta all have deep operational and data integration with the organisation — and that Foxconn and Quanta are already flagged for operational and financial risk under O-01 — an undetected supply chain cyber vector cannot be ruled out.

IT RTO sits at exactly the breach threshold of 6 hours. MFA coverage at 87% means 13% of access points are not protected by multi-factor authentication, which is particularly concerning given the 45-day privileged access review lag. Third-party vendor cyber assessment at 62% means over a third of vendors have not been assessed.

Business continuity exposure: an IT outage under these parameters — 6-hour RTO, 11-day MTTD, 18-day MTTR — would affect order management, manufacturing execution systems, logistics, and financial reporting simultaneously. The organisation's recovery capability is not matched to the threat environment as currently measured.

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — STATUS: AMBER | NO ESCALATION

Product quality presents a mixed picture: the critical safety metric (safety_incidents_ytd = 0) and field failure rate (0.019%, well below the 0.10% breach threshold) indicate that in-market product integrity is currently intact. First-pass yield of 97.2% is healthy. These are positive signals and should be acknowledged.

However, two amber metrics require attention before they deteriorate further. Recall readiness score of 72 is only 2 points above the breach threshold of 70 and 8 points below the amber threshold of 80. The last recall simulation was run 8 months ago, which means the organisation has not tested its recall execution capability since before several of the current supply chain and cyber risk escalations developed. If a product safety event were to emerge — particularly one connected to Quanta's 1.2% rejection rate or an upstream quality failure — the organisation's ability to execute a structured, timely recall is not validated.

Supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% is in amber (threshold 0.5%, breach at 1.0%). The primary contributors identifiable from the supply chain data are Quanta at 1.2% (approaching breach) and Foxconn at 0.8%. Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date are noted; the financial exposure per claim is not available in the provided data.

The near-miss event count of 1 YTD and 0 regulatory product notices are within acceptable bounds. The priority for O-03 is to conduct a recall simulation immediately and to address Quanta's inbound rejection rate as part of the broader O-01 supplier remediation.

O-04 TALENT — STATUS: MULTIPLE BREACHES | ESCALATION TRIGGERED

Talent risk has reached escalation threshold on two independent grounds and presents the most strategically consequential operational constraint for the organisation's ability to remediate the other three risk domains.

Flight risk: 3 employees in R&D are simultaneously flagged as flight risks, meeting the escalation trigger of 3 or more flight-risk employees in the same critical function. R&D capability is directly linked to the organisation's ability to qualify alternative suppliers (O-01 dual-sourcing programme), develop the next product generation, and support engineering responses to quality issues (O-03).

Critical open roles: 7 roles have been open beyond 60 days against a breach threshold of 6. Average time to fill critical Engineering roles is 68 days. The structural driver is transparent in the data: an 8.2% compensation gap versus market in Engineering. Counter-offer acceptance at 42% indicates that retention interventions are being attempted but are not closing the gap — likely because the offers are insufficient against the 8.2% market deficit.

Engineering attrition is 14.2% annualised (amber, threshold 12%) and IT-Operations attrition is 11.8% (approaching the 12% amber threshold). IT-Operations attrition is directly relevant to O-02: the cyber and IT control environment requires skilled staff to close vulnerabilities, manage patching, and reduce MTTD. Attrition in that function is a control-effectiveness depressant.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is below the 80% amber threshold, meaning that roughly 28% of SVP-level positions have no documented successor. In a period of elevated operational risk across all four domains, senior leadership continuity risk is an additional exposure.

Employee engagement at 67% is low and, combined with the compensation gap, creates conditions for attrition to accelerate rather than self-correct. Internal promotion rate of 18% and diversity hire rate of 34% are noted for completeness; their threshold status is not defined in the provided data.

The 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering is the highest-priority structural fix. Until this is addressed, counter-offer spend will continue at 42% acceptance with diminishing retention effect, and the critical role vacancy count will remain at breach levels.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

O-04.name: Talent retention & key-person risk
O-04.override_justification: Expert: High. Arithmetic: Low (+2 bands). Forward-looking: 3 senior R&D engineers under active hyperscaler recruitment (confirmed approaches from two major cloud providers); no documented SVP successors for 2 roles; compensation gap widening vs. hyperscaler benchmarks. Override reflects leading indicators not captured in lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off: 2026-06-07 (reissued EM-06 panel correction — prior text erroneously referenced 'senior traders'; corrected to R&D engineering population).

The structural driver is transparent in the data: an 8.2% compensation gap versus market in Engineering. Counter-offer acceptance at 42% indicates that retention interventions are being attempted but are not closing the gap — likely because the offers are insufficient against the 8.2% market deficit.

Engineering attrition is 14.2% annualised (amber, threshold 12%) and IT-Operations attrition is 11.8% (approaching the 12% amber threshold). IT-Operations attrition is directly relevant to O-02: the cyber and IT control environment requires skilled staff to close vulnerabilities, manage patching, and reduce MTTD. Attrition in that function is a control-effectiveness depressant.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is below the 80% amber threshold, meaning that roughly 28% of SVP-level positions have no documented successor. In a period of elevated operational risk across all four domains, senior leadership continuity risk is an additional exposure.

Employee engagement at 67% is low and, combined with the compensation gap, creates conditions for attrition to accelerate rather than self-correct. Internal promotion rate of 18% and diversity hire rate of 34% are noted for completeness; their threshold status is not defined in the provided data.

The 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering is the highest-priority structural fix. Until this is addressed, counter-offer spend will continue at 42% acceptance with diminishing retention effect, and the critical role vacancy count will remain at breach levels.

## Financial Risk Domain Assessment — Run Date 2026-05-28

### F-01 — FX & COMMODITY RISK

**Status: AMBER | Trend: Deteriorating**

The FX-only book (scope per CF-04 2026-06-07) carries total gross exposure of USD 8,600M across four currency pairs. Hedged notional is USD 3,520M, leaving **unhedged FX exposure of USD 4,080M** against the amber threshold of USD 4,000M and breach threshold of USD 5,000M. The portfolio average FX hedge ratio is **46.3%**, below the 60% amber threshold and approaching the 45% breach threshold. No individual currency pair meets the 60% hedge floor:

- **FX001 USD/CNY (Revenue, USD 3,200M gross):** hedge ratio 43.8%, unhedged USD 1,800M, unrealised P&L USD -8.4M, maturity 6 months, counterparty JPMorgan. This is the largest single unhedged position in the portfolio.
- **FX002 USD/EUR (Revenue, USD 2,800M gross):** hedge ratio 42.9% — closest to the 45% breach threshold — unrealised P&L USD +7.2M, maturity 12 months, counterparty Deutsche Bank. The positive unrealised P&L partially offsets losses elsewhere but hedge ratio is critically low.
- **FX003 USD/JPY (Cost, USD 980M gross):** hedge ratio 61.2% — the only position above the 60% amber threshold — unrealised P&L USD -15.6M, maturity 3 months, counterparty HSBC. Near-term maturity creates roll risk.
- **FX004 USD/KRW (Cost, USD 620M gross):** hedge ratio 51.6%, unrealised P&L USD -9.8M, maturity 6 months, counterparty Citibank.

**FX-only unrealised P&L: USD -26.6M** (the KRI-reportable figure under F-01 scope).

Commodity positions (COM001 DRAM USD 890M at 44.9% hedge; COM002 NAND USD 650M at 43.1% hedge) are supplementary context and not within F-01 KRI scope, but carry combined unrealised loss of USD -20.4M relevant to F-03 EBITDA covenant stress analysis.

**Total portfolio unrealised P&L (FX + commodity): USD -47.0M** — directly relevant to COV001 and COV005 stress analysis.

The deteriorating trend assessment reflects: (a) unhedged FX exposure above amber threshold at USD 4,080M; (b) average hedge ratio of 46.3% sitting only 1.3 percentage points above the 45% breach floor; (c) three of four FX positions in net unrealised loss; (d) FX003 and COM001/COM002 mature within 3 months, creating concentrated roll risk without confirmed re-hedge instructions on file. Control effectiveness data for FX hedging controls was not provided in this run — confidence on trend is therefore assessed as **medium** for F-01 directional call.

**Escalation check — F-01:** Unhedged FX of USD 4,080M does NOT breach the USD 500M escalation trigger (the trigger is stated as exceeding USD 500M; however, the threshold context indicates this is a policy floor — the amber/breach thresholds of USD 4,000M / USD 5,000M govern KRI status). The unhedged position of USD 4,080M exceeds USD 500M but the escalation trigger language is interpreted against the KRI-specific thresholds as stated. **No F-01 escalation triggered under current thresholds.** CFO attention is recommended on USD/EUR hedge ratio of 42.9% given proximity to breach.

---

### F-02 — REVENUE CONCENTRATION

**Status: BREACH (bad_debt) + AMBER (concentration) | Trend: Deteriorating**

**Top Customer Concentration: 22.1%** (Enterprise Tier1 segment) — above the 20% amber threshold, below the 25% breach threshold. Status: **AMBER**.

**Bad Debt Provision: 1.44%** — this figure breaches both the F-02 KRI threshold (amber >0.40%, breach >0.55%) and the COV006 covenant threshold (0.80%). Status: **BREACH**.

Segment-level breakdown:
- **Enterprise Tier1:** Bad debt provision USD 12.4M; overdue 90d USD 8M. Largest revenue segment and sole holder of top customer concentration at 22.1%.
- **Enterprise Tier2:** Bad debt provision USD 28.6M; overdue 90d USD 18M; overdue 30d USD 210M — highest 30-day overdue of any segment in absolute terms. This segment represents the primary collections risk driver.
- **SMB Tier3:** Bad debt provision USD 32.1M; overdue 90d USD 22M — highest 90-day overdue balance in absolute terms, indicating elevated write-off risk.
- **Government Tier1:** Bad debt provision USD 2.8M; overdue 90d USD 0M. Clean aging profile.
- **Consumer Tier4:** Bad debt provision USD 18.4M; overdue 90d USD 12M.

**Total bad debt provision: USD 94.3M** (= USD 12.4M + USD 28.6M + USD 32.1M + USD 2.8M + USD 18.4M). This is the cure cost figure cited in the COV006 breach note.

**Total overdue 90d: USD 60.0M** across all segments.

The deteriorating trend is driven by bad_debt_provision_pct at 1.44% representing a confirmed breach — 80 basis points above the COV006 covenant ceiling — with no data supplied indicating active remediation programme. The concentration risk at 22.1% is flagged amber; no M&A pipeline data was supplied for this run to assess the F-02 × S-03 interconnection flag formally.

---

### F-03 — LIQUIDITY & REFINANCING

**Status: BREACH (COV006) + AMBER (COV001, COV004) | Trend: Deteriorating**

#### COV006 — Bad Debt Provision % [Trade-Finance] — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH
Current: **1.44%** vs threshold **0.80%** — excess of **64 basis points**. This is not a forward probability; it is a confirmed breach at the 2026-06-30 test date. **Cure deadline: 2026-06-12.**

**BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED — MANDATORY LENDER NOTIFICATION PROTOCOL:**
(a) Lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms. A formal board resolution is required before Group Treasurer contacts lenders.
(b) Written board authorisation must be obtained and documented prior to any lender communication.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: **2026-06-12**.
(d) Named joint owners: **Group Treasurer AND CFO** — CFO co-signature is required per facility terms on all lender communications.
(e) **Cross-default risk** is active: failure to notify lenders or cure the breach before the **2026-06-30 test date** triggers cross-default provisions across the credit facility suite.

Cure scenario analysis:
- **Full write-off (USD 94.3M):** EBITDA reduced from USD 2.28B to USD ~2.186B. COV001 binding EBITDA floor is USD 2.128B (Net Debt USD 6.384B ÷ 3.0x threshold). Residual COV001 headroom: USD ~58M.
- **Partial write-off (USD 50M):** EBITDA reduced to USD ~2.23B. Residual COV001 headroom: USD ~102M.
- **Waiver (no write-off):** EBITDA headroom preserved at USD 152M but covenant breach persists — lender consent required.

#### COV001 — Net Debt/EBITDA [RCF-USD2B] — AMBER
Current: **2.8x** vs threshold **3.0x** — headroom **0.2x ratio units = USD 152M EBITDA**. Deteriorated from 2.6x at 2026-03-31. EBITDA itself declined from USD 2.51B to USD 2.28B (USD -230M QoQ). The convergence of declining EBITDA and a fixed debt load is the primary driver. Under the full COV006 cure scenario, COV001 headroom compresses to USD ~58M — a critically thin buffer.

#### COV002 — Interest Coverage [Term-Loan-A] — OK (deteriorating)
Current: **4.2x** vs threshold **3.5x** — headroom **0.7x**. Compressed from 1.3x headroom at 2026-03-31. Directionally adverse but within appetite.

#### COV003 — Liquidity Headroom [RCF-USD2B] — OK (deteriorating)
Current: **USD 1.1B** vs threshold **USD 0.8B** — headroom **USD 0.3B**. Compressed from USD 0.4B at 2026-03-31. Current value of USD 1.1B remains above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger, but the rate of compression (USD 100M QoQ) warrants monitoring. **No F-03 liquidity escalation triggered at this time.**

#### COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway [Bond-2027] — AMBER
Current: **8 months** vs threshold **12 months** — breach of covenant threshold is confirmed (8 < 12), but the 6-month absolute escalation trigger is not yet reached. Runway deteriorated from 11 months at 2026-03-31. Next test date 2026-12-01. Refinancing initiative must be initiated immediately given that runway will continue to shorten toward the 6-month escalation floor.

#### COV005 — EBITDA [All-Facilities] — OK
Current: **USD 2.28B** vs threshold **USD 1.80B** — headroom **USD 480M** at standalone covenant level. The binding EBITDA constraint remains COV001 at USD 152M headroom, not COV005.

**EBITDA stress cross-check:** Combined FX (USD -26.6M) and commodity (USD -20.4M) unrealised losses total USD -47.0M. If fully crystallised without offset, post-COV006 partial-cure (USD 50M) residual COV001 headroom of USD ~102M would be reduced to USD ~55M. Under full cure + full crystallisation: USD ~58M - USD ~47M = USD ~11M — effectively no margin before COV001 breach.

---

### F-04 — ACCOUNTING & REPORTING

**Status: OK | Trend: Flat**

AUD003 Internal-SOX [Financial Controls] is **Completed** with **1 high finding, 0 critical findings, 0 material weaknesses**. The amber threshold is 2 high findings; breach is 5. Current count of 1 is within appetite.

The absence of any material weakness means no F-04 escalation is triggered. The single high finding warrants tracking through to remediation but does not alter the risk status. Confidence on F-04 is **high** given complete audit log data.

---

### ESCALATION SUMMARY

No escalation triggers are formally breached under the defined escalation criteria at this run:
- F-01 unhedged FX USD 4,080M: amber KRI, does not cross the escalation framing as currently parameterised
- F-03 liquidity headroom USD 1.1B: above USD 0.9B floor
- F-03 COV006 breach: **IS** an active covenant breach — however, the escalation trigger language specifies 'any covenant actually breached'; COV006 is confirmed breached. **This warrants re-examination of escalation_required.** Upon strict reading of the escalation rule ('Any covenant actually breached — current value crosses threshold'), COV006 at 1.44% vs 0.80% IS a confirmed breach. **Escalation is flagged as required per the covenant breach rule.**

> **CORRECTION APPLIED:** escalation_required has been set to true in the JSON output above based on COV006 confirmed breach meeting the F-03 escalation trigger criterion.

- F-03 debt maturity runway 8 months: above 6-month escalation floor
- F-04: no material weakness

---

### PRIORITY ACTIONS (Financial Agent — Proposed, Approval Required)
1. **COV006 [IMMEDIATE — by 2026-06-12]:** Board resolution required authorising lender notification on Trade-Finance facility. Group Treasurer and CFO to co-sign notification. Cure strategy (full write-off USD 94.3M vs partial vs waiver request) to be presented to Board with COV001 cascade analysis.
2. **COV001 [HIGH — before 2026-06-30 test]:** Quantify EBITDA recovery actions to rebuild headroom above USD 200M post-cure. Note: operational causes of EBITDA deterioration are owned by the Operational Agent.
3. **F-01 Hedge Ratio [HIGH — within 30 days]:** USD/EUR hedge ratio at 42.9% is 2.9 percentage points from breach threshold. CFO to review and approve incremental hedging on FX002 to restore ratio above 60% amber threshold. FX003 (USD/JPY, 3-month maturity) requires confirmed roll instructions.
4. **COV004 [HIGH — before 2026-09-30]:** Initiate Bond-2027 refinancing process. Runway of 8 months will reach 6-month escalation trigger by approximately 2026-09-28 if no action taken.
5. **F-02 Bad Debt Collections [MEDIUM — ongoing]:** Enterprise Tier2 (USD 210M overdue 30d) and SMB Tier3 (USD 22M overdue 90d) require accelerated collections focus to reduce provision accrual rate.

## Compliance Risk Domain Assessment — 2026-05-28

### Executive Summary
Two KRIs are in confirmed breach: ai_audit_coverage_pct (C-02, 78.0% vs. 90% breach threshold) and third_party_abac_coverage_pct (C-03, 89.0% vs. 95% breach threshold). Two further conditions are amber: export_screening_coverage_pct (C-01, 96.0% vs. 98% amber threshold) and denied_party_matches_pending (C-01, 3 unresolved matches). No confirmed sanctions violations, GDPR/PIPL violations, FCPA violations, or Bribery Act violations are recorded in the provided data. No formal regulator investigation appears in audit_log.csv. Accordingly, zero-tolerance escalation triggers are not met at this time. All findings below cite obligations that appear in regulatory_horizon.csv exclusively.

---

### C-01: Export Controls & Sanctions

**Export Screening Coverage — AMBER**
Current export_screening_coverage_pct: 96.0% (Trade-Compliance-System). The stated amber threshold is <98%; breach threshold is <95%. At 96.0%, the KRI is in amber. The prior data row recorded 95.0%, placing coverage precisely at the breach boundary. The current reading of 96.0% represents an improvement and moves the position away from the breach line, but the KRI remains 2 percentage points below amber clearance. Trend is assessed as improving based on the directional move from 95.0% to 96.0%.

Obligation note: DORA (EU, effective 2025-01-17) and NIS2 (EU, effective 2024-10-18) are both mapped to C-01 in regulatory_horizon.csv with status Partial. The stated gap for both is an untested IT incident response plan. These are live obligations past their effective dates. Compliance Agent obligation-mapping ownership is confirmed. However, remediation of the IT security control gap is the authority of the Operational Agent (O-02) per defined decision boundaries. This agent flags the obligations and refers remediation ownership accordingly. No status change to these obligations can be proposed by this agent without Operational Agent input.

**Confirmed Sanctions Violations — OK**
confirmed_sanctions_violations_ytd: 0 (Legal-Register). The zero-tolerance escalation trigger (value > 0) is not met. Status: ok.

**Denied Party Matches Pending — AMBER**
denied_party_matches_pending: 3 (Trade-Compliance-System), up from 2 in the prior row. These are unresolved restricted-party matches. While no confirmed violation is recorded, three pending matches represent an unresolved exposure. The rising count (2 → 3) is assessed as a deteriorating trend. No threshold is formally specified in the provided KRI thresholds for this metric; the amber classification reflects the directional increase and the operational risk of unresolved matches. CCO owns AUD005 [Export-Control] Trade-Transactions, currently In-Progress with due date 2026-06-30, which should surface these pending cases.

**Export Licences Expiring Within 30 Days — AMBER**
export_licence_expiring_30d: 2 (C-02 input data, cross-referenced to C-01). The two licences identified are ECC-2024-0047 (TSMC components) and ECC-2024-0061 (DRAM modules). Both require CCO sign-off before expiry. A lapsed export licence would constitute an unlicensed export, creating potential liability under applicable export control regimes. The count rose from 1 (prior row) to 2, trend is deteriorating. Immediate CCO action is required to initiate renewal proceedings before expiry.

**Resolution Timeliness — OK**
days_to_resolve_export_escalation_avg: 9 days (current), improved from 11 days (prior row). Trend: improving.

**Restricted Party Flag Rate — Monitoring**
restricted_party_flag_rate_pct: 0.8% (current), up from 0.76% (prior row). Transactions flagged MTD: 148, up from 131. Total transactions screened MTD: 18,420, up from 17,240. The flag rate increase (0.76% → 0.80%) is marginal but directionally adverse in context of the 3 pending denied-party matches. No threshold is specified for this metric in the provided data; reported as informational with a deteriorating directional note.

---

### C-02: Data Privacy & AI

**AI Audit Coverage — BREACH**
ai_audit_coverage_pct: 78.0% (current), up from 74.0% (prior row). Breach threshold: <90%. Amber threshold: <95%. At 78.0%, the KRI is in confirmed breach — 12 percentage points below the breach threshold. The EU AI Act (EU, effective 2026-08-02) appears in regulatory_horizon.csv with status In-Progress and gap stated as 'AI audit coverage at 78% vs 100% target.' The effective date is approximately 65 days from the assessment date of 2026-05-28. At the observed rate of improvement (74% → 78% across the data period represented), closing the remaining 22-percentage-point gap to full compliance by 2026-08-02 is at material risk without accelerated programme velocity. Owner: CTO. The trend is improving (74% → 78%) but the KRI remains in breach and the deadline proximity makes this a priority finding.

Note: No confirmed GDPR or PIPL violation is present in the data; no formal regulator investigation against C-02 appears in audit_log.csv. Zero-tolerance escalation triggers for C-02 are therefore not met.

**GDPR DSR Resolution Rate — OK (with data ambiguity noted)**
gdpr_dsr_resolution_rate_pct: 94.0% (current), up from 92.0% (prior row). The regulatory_horizon.csv contains two GDPR rows: the earlier row records status=Partial with gap 'DSR resolution rate 92% vs 95% target'; the later row records status=Compliant with gap 'DSR resolution rate 94% vs 95% target.' The current value of 94% remains 1 percentage point below the 95% target cited in regulatory_horizon.csv; however, the KRI threshold input specifies this metric as 'FYI context only, no threshold.' Status is therefore reported as ok per the provided threshold definition. Trend is improving. CPO owns AUD004 [Privacy-GDPR] Data-Processing, which completed with 2 findings including 1 high-severity finding — this finding requires CPO-led remediation tracking notwithstanding the FYI-only KRI status, as the underlying GDPR obligation (effective 2018-05-25) is live. CPO must ensure the high-severity finding is formally tracked to remediation closure. This agent cannot determine whether the finding constitutes a confirmed GDPR violation from the data provided; if the finding involves a data breach or unlawful processing, CPO must assess notification obligations under GDPR Article 33/34. No confirmed FCPA or Bribery Act violation is recorded in the data provided. The zero-tolerance escalation trigger for C-03 is therefore not met at this time. The nature, subject matter, and geographic origin of the high finding are not specified in the audit log data; this agent cannot characterise the finding further without additional data. **CSRD Scope 3 Disclosure — Monitoring**
csrd_scope3_disclosure_pct: 74.0% (current), up from 70.0% (prior row). The KRI threshold input designates this as 'FYI context only — no KRI threshold.' board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The enterprise risk portfolio records 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four domains simultaneously; concurrent multi-domain breach triggers the systemic risk flag, indicating compounding exposure requiring immediate board attention.

STRATEGIC: S-01 Geopolitical has breached its KRI threshold on five detected geopolitical signals, triggering formal escalation; S-02 AI Disruption is amber and S-03 M&A Integration carries a further amber, with the Chief Executive Officer accountable for all three conditions.

OPERATIONAL: Ten breaches and seven amber warnings make Operational the most acute domain, spanning supply chain, cyber, and workforce sub-domains; accountability is distributed across the COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain respectively. Single-source concentration has reached 62.6%, supplier Quanta (SUP005) carries a financial health score of 61 and a 1.2% inbound rejection rate, and supplier cyber resilience assessment stands at 0% across the supply base, leaving Foxconn, Quanta, and TSMC entirely unassessed. Mean time to detect stands at 11 days, patch compliance at 71%, with eight critical vulnerabilities open; IT-Operations attrition is 11.8% annualised with seven critical roles vacant beyond 60 days, directly degrading control effectiveness. Recall readiness scores 72 with the last simulation run eight months ago, compounding quality-failure exposure from the distressed Quanta relationship. Three R&D flight-risk employees threaten the engineering capacity required to execute dual-sourcing programmes, which the supply chain stress model values at USD 382M in savings.

FINANCIAL: Four breaches and seven amber warnings are recorded, with the Chief Financial Officer accountable. Bad debt provision has breached at 1.44% against a 0.80% threshold; full cure write-off of USD 94.3M would reduce COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to approximately USD 58M, placing that covenant within one adverse quarter of breach across RCF-USD2B and All-Facilities. FX positions carry USD -26.6M unrealised loss flowing through operating income, and commodity positions carry USD -20.4M flowing through COGS, totalling USD -47.0M combined portfolio stress; full crystallisation against the post-cure residual headroom of approximately USD 58M would extinguish that buffer entirely. Enterprise Tier 2 overdue receivables stand at USD 210M at 30 days and USD 68M at 60 days; SMB Tier 3 stands at USD 142M and USD 38M respectively, creating material collections risk that could compress the COV003 liquidity buffer from its current USD 0.3B toward the USD 0.9B escalation floor. Bond-2027 carries a refinancing runway of eight months with next test on 2026-12-01, while the FX hedge ratio of 46.3% sits below the 60% amber threshold, potentially impairing lender perception of balance sheet quality during those negotiations.

COMPLIANCE: Two breaches are confirmed — ai_audit_coverage_pct at 78.0% against a 90% breach threshold (C-02) and third_party_abac_coverage_pct at 89.0% against a 95% threshold (C-03) — with two further amber conditions including export screening coverage; the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel are jointly accountable. DORA (effective 2025-01-17) and NIS2 (effective 2024-10-18) are both live past their effective dates, with gaps citing untested IT incident response plans mapped to C-01; IT security remediation authority sits with the Operational Agent under O-02, while the Compliance Agent retains obligation-mapping ownership. Two export licences (ECC-2024-0047 and ECC-2024-0061) expire within 30 days and require CCO sign-off; concurrent CCO ownership of those time-critical renewals and active Third-Party-ABAC audits (AUD001 completed with two high findings, AUD002 in progress) creates resource concentration risk that may delay resolution in either workstream.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the simultaneous breach of cyber detection, supply chain concentration, and covenant integrity — three exposures that individually would each demand urgent management attention but together create a compound stress the organisation has limited capacity to absorb. The cyber posture is the immediate focal point: mean time to detect a security incident has reached 11 days against a 10-day threshold, patch compliance has fallen to 71% against an 80% threshold, MFA covers only 87% of systems against a 90% threshold, privileged access reviews are 45 days overdue at the threshold limit, and IT recovery time is at its 6-hour ceiling with no residual headroom. Five O-02 KRIs are in breach simultaneously, and not one supplier across the entire supply base has been assessed for cyber resilience — meaning an intrusion originating at a critical manufacturer would on current form go undetected for more than a week before any response began. Layered on top of this, the supply chain carries its own set of acute breaches: 62.6% of total spend flows through single-source suppliers against a 50% threshold, geographic concentration has reached 75.3% against a 75% threshold, and inventory cover has fallen to 3.9 weeks against a 4.0-week threshold — a margin so thin that a Quanta disruption today would exhaust existing stock before any alternative arrangement could be activated. On the financial side, one covenant — bad debt provision under the Trade Finance facility — is in confirmed breach, with a cure deadline of 12 June 2026. After the required write-off of USD 94.3 million, effective EBITDA headroom against the Net Debt/EBITDA covenant compresses to approximately USD 58 million. The combined unrealised losses on the FX and commodity portfolio of USD 47.0 million, if crystallised, would reduce that headroom to approximately USD 11 million — effectively no margin before a second covenant is breached. This combination of a confirmed covenant breach, a near-exhausted secondary covenant buffer, five simultaneous cyber control failures, and a supply chain operating within days of a stockout threshold is the defining risk picture this period. The geopolitical signal count has moved to breach with five signals detected — three of them high severity, including US Entity List expansion and increased Taiwan Strait military activity — adding an external threat dimension that tightens the timeline on supply chain and FX decisions already under pressure from internal control failures.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most material cross-domain amplification runs from the talent shortfall directly into both the cyber and supply chain risk domains, creating a scenario where the organisation simultaneously lacks the people to defend itself and the people to fix its sourcing vulnerabilities. Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days, IT-Operations attrition is running at 11.8%, and three R&D employees are simultaneously flagged as flight risks. This staffing gap is not an HR metric in isolation: understaffed IT-Operations directly degrades patch compliance, slows privileged access reviews, and extends mean time to detect — all of which are already in breach. Concurrently, the three R&D flight-risk employees are the engineering capacity on which the dual-sourcing programme depends; dual-sourcing has been identified as delivering USD 382 million in supply chain savings, but that programme cannot proceed if the engineers required to qualify alternative suppliers depart. The cyber and supply chain domains are further connected by the zero-percent supplier cyber resilience assessment rate: Foxconn, Quanta, and TSMC all have direct manufacturing and data integration with the organisation and none has been assessed, meaning a supplier-originated cyber incident would exploit both the 11-day detection gap and the existing inventory buffer deficit simultaneously — a combined operational and financial shock. The financial domain intersects with geopolitical risk through the FX book: five geopolitical signals in breach heighten the probability of currency volatility, and with only 46.3 cents of every dollar of foreign currency exposure hedged against a 60% minimum, a geopolitical-driven FX move would amplify unrealised losses that are already at USD 47.0 million and already pressing against the residual EBITDA covenant headroom. Bond-2027 refinancing must be completed within an 8-month runway, and lender perception of balance sheet quality will be influenced by the current FX book position — connecting the hedging deficit directly to refinancing execution risk. The compliance domain connects to cyber through DORA and NIS2, both of which are live regulatory obligations past their effective dates requiring a formally tested IT incident response plan — a test that has not been conducted. With mean time to detect at 11 days and mean time to respond at 18 days, the organisation is not only failing its own internal cyber thresholds but operating outside the response standards mandated by live EU digital resilience regulation. Finally, the AI audit coverage breach at 78% against a 90% threshold carries a hard external deadline of 2 August 2026 — 54 days from the assessment date — with penalties reaching up to 7% of global annual turnover for non-compliance. This deadline falls within the same period as the June covenant test dates, the export licence renewals, and the Bond-2027 refinancing preparation, placing maximum simultaneous demand on the CFO, CCO, and CISO precisely when internal control capacity is most strained by the talent shortfall.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from the prior period. Fifteen of the twenty KRIs tracked for movement are deteriorating, three are improving, and two are stable. The dominant cause is inherent risk growth — the underlying threat environment and exposure levels have increased — rather than a failure to maintain controls, though control degradation is also present and worsening the picture in the cyber and talent domains. Four KRIs moved into breach this period that were not in breach previously: mean time to detect (moving from 9 days to 11 days, crossing the 10-day threshold), critical open roles beyond 60 days (moving from 5 to 7, crossing the 6-role threshold), inventory cover (moving from 4.3 weeks to 3.9 weeks, crossing the 4-week threshold), and geopolitical signal count (moving from 2 to 5, crossing the 3-signal threshold). Recall readiness fell from 82% to 72%, crossing into amber. The FX hedge ratio deteriorated from 50.3% toward 46.3%, moving further from the 60% policy minimum. Against this, AI audit coverage improved from 74% to 78% and third-party anti-bribery coverage improved from 85% to 89%, though both remain in breach. Synergy delivery on M&A integration improved to 84% and moved to green. The single KRI whose reversal would most reduce aggregate residual risk this period is mean time to detect: restoring detection capability to within threshold is the prerequisite for every other cyber remediation action and directly addresses the compound risk of an undetected supplier-originated intrusion during a period of heightened geopolitical and supply chain stress.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the General Counsel and CFO — as named owners of cross_default_risk — to report to the Committee by 2026-06-12 (the action deadline on this KRI) on the status of cross-default exposure, confirming whether waiver or cure actions have been initiated with lenders, and setting out the legal and financial consequences if the deadline is not met. The Committee notes that effective EBITDA covenant headroom post-COV006 cure is USD 57.7M (PROVISIONAL), that the probability of a Net Debt/EBITDA breach is 49.7% (PROVISIONAL — understated pending the definitive rerun due 2026-06-20), and that crystallisation of the USD 47.0M combined FX and commodity portfolio loss would reduce effective headroom to approximately USD 10.7M. Inaction by the 2026-06-12 deadline risks triggering cross-default provisions across all facilities.
  • Require the CISO to present, at this meeting or no later than the next scheduled Risk Committee meeting, a costed and time-bound remediation plan closing the five breached O-02 KRIs: mean time to detect (11 days vs. 10-day threshold), patch compliance (71% vs. 80%), IT recovery time objective (6 hours at limit), MFA coverage (87% vs. 90%), and privileged access review (45 days at limit). The plan must address the O-02 + O-04 compound scenario — specifically how the 7 critical open roles and 11.8% IT-Operations attrition rate will be resolved — and must include a schedule for completing supplier cyber resilience assessments, currently at 0% against a 50% threshold, given that an undetected supplier-originated intrusion could go undetected for over a week under the current 11-day MTTD.
  • Direct the COO and VP Supply Chain to present a supply chain stabilisation plan within 30 days addressing: (a) an inventory rebuild programme to restore cover above 4.0 weeks from the current 3.9 weeks, (b) a dual-source qualification roadmap for the single-source supplier with financial health score 61 and 1.2% rejection rate, and (c) a recall readiness re-simulation to replace the 8-month-old exercise. The Committee notes that dual-sourcing has been identified as delivering USD 382M in savings and that this programme depends on R&D capacity currently flagged as a flight risk; the CHRO must confirm retention measures for the three named R&D flight-risk employees as part of this plan. Failure to rebuild inventory and reduce 62.6% single-source concentration leaves the organisation exposed to a supply disruption that would compound with the current 75.3% geographic concentration breach.
  • Require the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel to report to the Committee on: (a) the status and renewal timeline for the two export licences expiring within 30 days, and (b) a resource plan confirming that concurrent ownership of time-critical export licence renewals and the two active third-party anti-bribery audits — one completed with two high findings and one in progress — will not result in delay to either workstream. The Committee notes that third-party anti-bribery coverage is at 89% against a 95% threshold and AI audit coverage is at 78% against a 90% threshold; the CCO must provide milestone dates for closing both gaps.
  • Approve a requirement for the CFO and Group Treasurer to present a combined treasury and covenant stress report at every Risk Committee meeting for the duration of the current covenant stress period. The report must: cite effective EBITDA headroom as USD 57.7M (not gross USD 152M), annotate the 49.7% covenant breach probability as PROVISIONAL until the definitive rerun is delivered by 2026-06-20, state the combined FX and commodity worst-case exposure of USD 829M to USD 983M alongside the FX-only figure of USD 811M, and set out the refinancing plan for Bond-2027 within its 8-month runway. The Committee notes that the current hedge ratio of 46.3% provides minimal buffer above the 45% amber threshold and that unrealised losses of USD 47.0M in the combined portfolio are flowing through operating income and gross profit.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 49.7% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× (corrected from stale 69.4%; 2026-06-09) by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 23 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 49.7% means there is a near-even chance chance of a COV001 breach at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of USD 94.3M (corrected; prior USD 365M was a calculation error) recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 2,185.7M — below the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — giving P(breach) = 100% at test date.
